common_name: Black Mondo Grass (Nigrescens Mondo Grass, Black Lily Turf)

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in spring in partial to full shade with moist, well-drained, slightly acidic soil; Black Mondo Grass establishes slowly but requires minimal maintenance once settled. It spreads gradually by short stolons and can be divided every 3–4 years. In zone 9, it performs as an evergreen groundcover; the jet-black color is most intense in part sun — in deep shade the foliage becomes dark green to very dark purple. Best color in winter.

directions: Plant in partial to full shade in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil; Black Mondo Grass performs best with morning sun or dappled light — too much shade causes the foliage to become dark green rather than the dramatic near-black that makes this plant so striking. Ophiopogon planiscapus 'Nigrescens' is one of the most distinctive groundcovers available for temperate and subtropical gardens, producing tufts of strap-shaped foliage in an extraordinary deep purple-black color — one of the darkest foliage colors achievable in a garden plant. It makes an electrifying contrast against silver, chartreuse, or white-flowering plants. The plant also produces pale pink to white flower spikes in summer followed by dark blue-black berries that persist through winter. Though slow-growing, Black Mondo Grass is an exceptionally long-lived, low-maintenance groundcover that requires virtually no care once established in appropriate conditions. In zone 9 it remains evergreen, spreading gradually to form a dense, weed-suppressing carpet. The named cultivar 'Nigrescens' (sometimes listed as O. planiscapus 'Arabicus' or 'Black Knight') consistently produces the darkest foliage; seedlings can be quite variable.
sources: Royal Horticultural Society — Ophiopogon planiscapus 'Nigrescens' (AGM plant)
North Carolina State Extension Horticulture — Ophiopogon planiscapus
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Ophiopogon species for Florida
Dirr, M.A. (2009) Manual of Woody Landscape Plants — Ophiopogon

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in spring in partial to full shade; Creeping Lilyturf spreads aggressively by underground stolons and fills in quickly to form a dense groundcover — it is more invasive and faster-spreading than the clumping Liriope muscari and can be difficult to contain. Best used in large areas, under trees, or on slopes where spreading is desired. Mow to 2–3 inches in late winter to remove old foliage before spring flush. Very tolerant of the dry, root-competitive shade under large live oaks.

directions: Plant in full sun to deep shade in any well-drained soil; Creeping Lilyturf is one of the most adaptable and drought-tolerant groundcovers available, thriving in the challenging conditions of dry shade under large trees, compacted soil, slopes, and other difficult sites where few groundcovers survive. Liriope spicata is closely related to and often confused with Big Blue Lilyturf (L. muscari), but the two plants differ in growth habit: L. muscari forms discrete clumps while L. spicata spreads aggressively by underground stolons to form a continuous carpet, making it much better suited as a large-area groundcover but much harder to control in garden beds. In zone 9, the narrow, grass-like, dark-green leaves remain evergreen through most winters; the plant produces spikes of pale lavender to nearly white flowers in late summer, followed by black berries. The spreading habit, extreme shade tolerance, and minimal care requirements make it the standard groundcover under Gulf Coast live oaks and large shade trees across the region. The cultivar 'Silver Dragon' (green-and-white striped leaves) provides bright variegation in shade.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Liriope species comparison
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Liriope spicata for Texas landscapes
Clemson Cooperative Extension — Liriope: Lilyturf and Monkey Grass
LSU AgCenter — Shade Groundcovers for Louisiana

common_name: Rainbow Spike Moss (Peacock Moss, Iridescent Spike Moss)

planting.note: Plant in partial to full shade in consistently moist, humus-rich soil; Rainbow Spike Moss requires higher humidity and more consistent moisture than most groundcovers and will desiccate quickly in dry conditions. It spreads by creeping stems to form a flat mat; space plants 6–12 inches apart for groundcover use. In zone 9, it performs as an evergreen groundcover in shaded, moist garden spots; it may brown in cold winters but usually resprouts from stem nodes. Excellent under tree fern canopies, in rain gardens, and as a terrarium plant.

directions: Plant in partial to full shade in consistently moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil; Rainbow Spike Moss requires more consistent moisture than most groundcovers and will rapidly crisp and die if allowed to dry out. Selaginella uncinata is a primitive vascular plant (a club moss or spike moss, not a true moss) from southern China that produces a remarkably iridescent, structural blue-green sheen on its tiny, overlapping scale-like leaves — an optical interference effect caused by thin-film photonic structures in the leaf cells, similar to the iridescence of a peacock feather or oil film on water. In low light conditions, the iridescence intensifies to an electric blue-teal that creates a jewel-like effect in the shade garden. The plant creeps flat along the ground, building overlapping mats of fern-like, two-dimensional fronds only 1–3 inches high. In zone 9 Gulf Coast gardens with reliable moisture — under tree canopies, in permanently shaded bed corners, in rain garden margins — Rainbow Spike Moss creates an extraordinary low carpet of living iridescence unlike any other groundcover available. It is equally prized as a vivarium, terrarium, and paludarium plant where its spectacular appearance and humidity requirements make it ideal for closed or semi-closed planted glass environments.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Selaginella species for Florida
American Fern Society — Selaginella uncinata cultivated notes
Floridata Plant Encyclopedia — Selaginella uncinata
LSU AgCenter — Groundcovers for Shaded Humid Louisiana Gardens

common_name: Orange Daylily (Tawny Daylily, Tiger Daylily, Ditch Lily)

planting.note: Plant divisions (fans) in spring or fall in full sun to partial shade; Orange Daylily adapts to a wide range of soils but blooms most profusely in full sun. It spreads vigorously by stolons and fleshy roots and can become invasive in ideal conditions — mow or divide regularly to control spread. In zone 9, it blooms May–June; in hot summers the foliage goes semi-dormant. Division every 3–5 years maintains vigor. The species form is triploid and produces no viable seed; it spreads only vegetatively.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in almost any well-drained soil; Orange Daylily is among the most adaptable and indestructible of all garden perennials, tolerating clay, sand, drought, and competition from tree roots while spreading steadily by stolons. Hemerocallis fulva is native to Asia and has been cultivated in gardens for over two thousand years, spreading throughout Europe and North America so thoroughly that it is now naturalized along roadsides, railroad embankments, and woodland edges across most of the continent. The large, bright orange, lily-like flowers — each bloom lasting just one day (hence 'day lily') but produced in succession over 3–4 weeks — bloom in May–June in zone 9. The plant has naturalized so widely in North America because it is triploid and cannot produce viable seed; all spread is vegetative, but each original planting can slowly colonize a large area. Despite its commonness, the orange daylily remains a beautiful garden plant — the species form provides a reliable, bold orange bloom in late spring when few other perennials are at peak. The tender young shoots and flower buds are edible and used as a vegetable in Chinese cuisine (golden needles or 'gum jum'). Young daylily buds are also used in moo shu pork and hot-and-sour soup.
sources: American Hemerocallis Society — Hemerocallis fulva species notes
USDA Plants Database — Hemerocallis fulva
Floridata Plant Encyclopedia — Hemerocallis fulva
Duke, J.A. (1992) Handbook of Edible Weeds — Hemerocallis fulva culinary uses

common_name: Autograph Tree (Pitch Apple, Balsam Apple, Florida Clusia)

planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens in late spring in full sun to partial shade in any well-drained soil; Autograph Tree is extremely salt-tolerant and grows vigorously in coastal Gulf Coast conditions including salt spray, sandy soils, and high humidity. In zone 9a it is frost-sensitive (damaged below 32°F) and should be planted in sheltered locations or used as a large container specimen. In zone 9b–11, it is a reliable evergreen landscape shrub or small tree. The thick leaves are so firm that initials scratched into them remain permanently visible — hence 'Autograph Tree.'

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in sandy, rocky, or average well-drained soil; Autograph Tree is exceptionally drought- and salt-tolerant once established, making it one of the premier coastal shrubs for zone 10–11 Gulf Coast and South Florida landscapes. Clusia rosea is a tropical tree from the Caribbean and tropical Americas that has become one of the most widely planted landscape shrubs in South Florida, the Keys, and coastal South Texas for its bold, glossy, ovate, extremely thick, leathery dark-green leaves and tolerance of the most challenging coastal growing conditions. The leaves' remarkable rigidity — they feel like synthetic material — allows text or drawings scratched with a nail or key to remain permanently etched as the leaf expands and matures, making it a natural curiosity and the source of the common name 'Autograph Tree.' In late summer, the plant produces attractive cup-shaped white to rose-pink flowers followed by fleshy green fruits that split open to reveal red seeds — both attractive to birds. Clusia rosea develops aerial roots that eventually reach the ground, giving mature specimens a striking strangler-fig appearance. The milky white latex sap was historically used as a boat caulk ('pitch') by Caribbean peoples — hence the name 'Pitch Apple.' A large, fast-growing hedge or specimen plant for zone 10–11.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Clusia rosea (Autograph Tree)
Floridata Plant Encyclopedia — Clusia rosea
Miami-Dade County Urban Forestry — Clusia species landscape use
Morton, J.F. (1987) Fruits of Warm Climates — Clusia rosea historical uses
